lines.text: 04:00. We're {{ELAPSED}} in. This next one — Against Me!, 'Sink, Florida, Sink' off New Wave. 2007. Laura Jane Grace wrote this one thinking about home the way you think about a house that's burning down and you're too far to do anything about it.

Grace started that band in Naples, Florida in '97. Gainesville became the real home, though. You move somewhere for the music scene and it becomes part of your bones. Then you write a song about it sinking into the ocean and you mean it literally and you mean it every other way too.

James Bowman's been with her since 2001. That's a long time to stay in the same room with someone. That's marriage in the punk sense — you're in a van together, you're fighting every night, you're making something that matters. By 2007 they'd already moved to Sire Records. Big label. But Grace kept writing like it was a basement demo.
The whole world is against me — that's what the band name means. That's what it still means. Some nights I think that's just what it takes to make something real.

Here's The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1997. Off their self-titled Epitaph debut. And yeah, Epitaph gave them a record deal and yeah, they were a rush job — the Souls will tell you that themselves. Songs that didn't feel finished. But sometimes songs that aren't finished yet are the ones that finish you.

Four kids from Basking Ridge, New Jersey who went to high school together and decided to move to New Brunswick. That's the punk move — you find three people you trust and you move to a college town and you build something. Greg, Pete, Bryan — they knew each other since they were kids. That matters.

They called their fans 'True Believers.' Not customers. Not metrics. Believers. They built Chunksaah Records themselves. Organized grassroots shows. Did the work nobody else wanted to do. And the fans came back because they knew the Souls actually gave a shit.

'Ole' is a singalong. It's the kind of song that sounds better with other people. Right now, some of you listening are singing along to this. I know you are. That's what the Souls built. That's what they still own.

tracks.title: A Better Place, A Better Time

lines.text: Hot Water Music. 'A Better Place, A Better Time.' 1997. Gainesville, Florida. Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard doing the call-and-response thing. Jason Black on bass. George Rebelo on drums. Four guys who got frustrated with their hometowns and decided to build something in Gainesville instead.

They named the band after a Charles Bukowski short-story collection. Hot Water Music. That's the name of a book you read when you're trying to find permission to be angry at the world and still make something beautiful. Bukowski gave them that.

1998 — and this is the part that kills me — they were so exhausted they broke up. They got to their farewell show in Gainesville and during the set, Ragan told the crowd, 'I don't know what to tell you guys... we're not keeping it going.' They meant it. They spent three months apart. And then they rehearsed for the goodbye and something happened. They started writing new songs. By the time they played that show, they'd decided to stay together.
That's what friendship sounds like in punk. You get to the edge and you either fall or you remember why you came here. They remembered.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' 1997. And yeah, it's a cover — that's what they do. They're a supergroup of people from other bands who got together to cover songs. Fat Mike from NOFX, Joey Cape from Lagwagon, Chris Shiflett from No Use for a Name. They came together to play covers during off-time because they wanted to have fun.

Their first album, Have a Ball, came out in '97. Pop hits from the '60s and '70s played at punk speed with punk fury. The philosophy was simple: take something that exists, break it down, rebuild it, make it mean something different. Make it yours.

They wore costumes on stage. Matching outfits. Quirky shit. You know what that is? That's freedom. That's people who've already made it with their real bands deciding to play dress-up and cover songs because the joke and the music are the same thing.
'Sunday Morning.' It's soft. It's a break. It's what you need to hear at 04:00 when everything else is loud. They took a song that existed and made it so you could breathe.

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. Off Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly and Chris McCaughan trading vocals. And that album title — Apathy and Exhaustion — that's what you get when you've been running on fumes for three years straight.

Kelly and McCaughan lived in an apartment complex called The Lawrence Arms. They got evicted in the middle of the night. So they named their band after it. That's the punk move — you take the thing that hurt you and you make it your name and you make it mean something. You don't let it stay small.

By 2002, Fat Mike had signed them to Fat Wreck Chords. They were on Warped Tour. They were supposed to be climbing. And Kelly was writing about Chicago like it was a place he was trying to remember. Their songs had footnotes. They cited history and literature. They were trying to say something bigger than 'I'm angry.'

Kelly's said in interviews that a lot of their experience came from being influenced by alcohol. That's honesty. That's knowing who you are and writing about it anyway. 'Coming Home' is what that sounds like — the sound of somebody who knows the way back is the hardest part.

Last one. NOFX. 'The Decline.' 1999. Eighteen minutes. Single track. And I'm going to let this one ride because if you're still listening at 04:00, you deserve to sit with something that takes up space.

Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin — those three have been together since Fairfax High School in Los Angeles. 1983. That's 16 years of writing together by the time 'The Decline' came out. Sixteen years of learning how to say something that matters in a format that nobody's supposed to take seriously.

The song is a fiery, cynical social commentary. That's the description. But what it really is — it's Fat Mike writing a letter to everyone he knew was listening. It's the sound of somebody who's watched the punk rock revival happen, watched MTV try to sell it back to people, and decided to spend eighteen minutes explaining why the whole thing was rigged.

They never signed to a major label, you know that? They were on Epitaph. They built their own label — Fat Wreck Chords — so they could own what they made. 'The Decline' is the sound of that independence. It's the sound of people who built something because they had to, not because they wanted to get rich.

So here we are. {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge. Two hours until the light comes back. This song is eighteen minutes. That means when it's done, we're that much closer to morning. That means we made it this far. That means the worst is behind us, or it's coming, but either way we're still here.

tickerItems.text: Madison YoY tracker: 165 kills at this hour last year. Current projection tracking 4% above. East Side holding at 34. State Street ruins: final incident count confirmed at 19.
Phone lines busier than anyone remembers. 47 callers on hold. Tim says: 'We're taking what we can. Stay patient. You're not alone.'
North Side anomaly continues — no new incidents past 02:30. Reason still unknown. Holden's running pattern analysis if he stays awake.
